ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Cucur Bawang. The broader fritter class FOODON_03315671 is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Cucur Bawang. A0CKP (deep-fried dough-based snack) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay snack food.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Cucur Bawang is approximately 80 g, equivalent to 3–4 bite-sized fritters as commonly served at Malaysian and Singaporean roadside stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Selected Street Foods and Snacks in Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB entry for Cucur Bawang was located. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore data for comparable deep-fried wheat-flour-based savoury fritters and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried dough and onion fritter analogues. Macronutrient profile is consistent with the described recipe (wheat flour, onion, oil, salt). Input record values from enriched_json (confidence 0.9) are retained where consistent with approved-source analogues.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cucur Bawang. GI of 70 is estimated by analogy to deep-fried wheat-flour-based fritters and pakoras (GI ~65–72) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The primary ingredient is refined wheat flour, a rapidly digestible starch; deep-frying adds fat which moderately slows digestion but does not substantially lower GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 27.6) / 100 = 19.3 g (high GL).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) and significant total fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Cucur Bawang intake due to its high sodium content (380 mg/100 g; approximately 304 mg per 80 g serving), which contributes meaningfully toward the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; occasional small portions are preferable to regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~70) and high refined carbohydrate load from wheat flour batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cucur Bawang has a high GI (~70) and a high glycemic load per serving (~19.3), making it likely to cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit portion size and pair with low-GI foods to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and potentially trans/oxidised fat content; high sodium adds cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process elevates total fat content (~12.5 g/100 g) and may introduce oxidised lipids depending on oil quality and reuse; combined with high sodium (380 mg/100 g), regular consumption is inadvisable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, and intake should be limited to occasional small servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cucur Bawang is a traditional Malay deep-fried savoury fritter popular in Malaysia and Singapore as a street food, roadside snack, and homemade teatime treat. The fritters are made from a simple batter of wheat flour, water, and salt, generously mixed with thinly sliced onions as the primary flavouring and texture element. Chopped scallions, Chinese celery, or a pinch of turmeric for colour are common additions. The batter is dropped by spoonfuls into hot oil and fried until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains soft, airy, and sweet from the caramelised onions. They are almost always served hot, accompanied by a spicy chilli dipping sauce or peanut sauce. Per 100 g, Cucur Bawang provides approximately 265 kcal, 4.2 g protein, 12.5 g fat, and 34.5 g carbohydrates, with 380 mg sodium. The high refined carbohydrate content and deep-frying method give it a high glycemic index (~70) and a high glycemic load per typical 80 g serving (~19.3). It is vegetarian and vegan but contains gluten. Clinically, it is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension due to its high GI, high sodium, and elevated fat content from frying. It is a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease. Cucur Bawang is best enjoyed occasionally and in small portions as part of a balanced diet.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB or IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Cucur Bawang was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore data for comparable deep-fried wheat-flour savoury fritters and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogues. The input record values (confidence 0.9, enriched_json) are consistent with approved-source analogues and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar deep-fried wheat-flour fritters. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Cucur Bawang contains no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero for this plant-based and flour-based fried snack. Confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous plant-based fried dough products. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): No animal-derived or fortified ingredients are present; vitamin D is naturally zero for this product. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for Cucur Bawang are not available in the HPB Singapore food database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific traditional preparation as of 2024. Saturated fat estimated at 1.8 g/100 g based on typical vegetable frying oil composition for analogous deep-fried snacks (HPB Singapore / USDA SR Legacy). 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Cucur Bawang was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are broader/analogous matches; no exact ontology code exists for Cucur Bawang.
